faqs.a: A prodrome is the early phase of an illness, when subtle changes appear before the full set of symptoms arrives. In mental health it's the quiet warning period before a major episode, like the weeks of sliding sleep, withdrawal, and irritability that can come before mania or a return of psychosis.

faqs.q: Why does recognizing a prodrome matter?
faqs.a: Spotting a prodrome creates a chance to act early. A treatment plan might call for contacting a clinician, adjusting medication, lowering stress, or protecting sleep the moment early signs appear, which can sometimes blunt an episode or keep it from fully developing.

faqs.q: How can I learn my own early warning signs?
faqs.a: People with episodic conditions and their families can work with a clinician to map out a personal list of warning signs ahead of time. Naming the pattern in advance helps because a prodrome is often clearest in hindsight rather than in the moment.
